message:
test(simulation): add rollout-gate verdict scenario covering search-ready + publish-crash + restart-recovery

Extend the search-asset simulation harness with an integration-style
scenario that exercises the three gates the rollout planner cares
about in a single run: search-ready threshold compliance, fail-open
behaviour while a repair is active, and publish-time crash recovery.

`rollout_gate_verdict_persists_thresholds_and_recovery_evidence`:
- Harness constructed with a four-beat `LoadScript`
  (idle / busy / loaded / idle) so each contention turn lands on a
  realistic machine-capacity sample.
- Installs a one-shot failpoint
  `FailpointId::Publish(PublishCrashWindow::SwapPublishedGeneration)`
  with `FailpointEffect::CrashOnce` — the restart path will pick up
  the partial swap.
- `ContentionPlan` drives four turns:
    1. `LexicalRepair`: build a search-ready generation and persist
       a `search_ready_gate` snapshot that records the observed
       1200ms vs 5000ms threshold with `status="pass"`.
    2. `ForegroundSearch`: query while the repair is running;
       assert the hybrid-fail-open contract fields
       (`requested_search_mode="hybrid"`,
       `realized_search_mode="lexical"`,
       `visible_generation="old_good"`) are persisted to the
       `fail_open_during_repair` snapshot.
    3. `LexicalRepair`: trigger the swap-publish failpoint — the
       generation gets mid-swapped, harness records the crash
       telemetry.
    4. `LexicalRepair`: simulate the post-restart verdict. The
       recovery path must restore the prior generation or roll
       the new one forward; the rollout-gate snapshot records
       which outcome landed with full crash evidence
       (failpoint_id, effect, crash_window).
- Verifies the persisted artifacts end up in the expected harness
  snapshot dir so the downstream rollout-gate consumer can read
  them back.

`.beads/issues.jsonl` + `.beads/last-touched` touch from br sync.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-8nnu","title":"Implement CDN integration with graceful fallback","description":"# Task: CDN Integration with Graceful Fallback

## Context
Enhance exported HTML with CDN-loaded libraries while ensuring the document
remains fully functional offline.

## CDN Resources

### 1. Tailwind CSS (Styling Enhancement)
```html
<!-- CDN with fallback detection -->
<script src="https://cdn.tailwindcss.com" defer 
    onerror="document.body.classList.add('no-tailwind')"></script>
```

Inline fallback styles activated when Tailwind fails to load.

### 2. Prism.js (Syntax Highlighting)
```html
<!-- Theme -->
<link rel="stylesheet" 
    href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/prismjs@1/themes/prism-tomorrow.min.css"
    media="print" onload="this.media='all'">

<!-- Core + Autoloader -->
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/prismjs@1/prism.min.js" defer></script>
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/prismjs@1/plugins/autoloader/prism-autoloader.min.js" defer></script>
```

### 3. JetBrains Mono Font (Optional Enhancement)
```html
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com">
<link rel="stylesheet" 
    href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=JetBrains+Mono:wght@400;500&display=swap"
    media="print" onload="this.media='all'">
```

## Fallback Strategy

### CSS Fallbacks
```css
/* Inline critical styles that cover Tailwind absence */
.no-tailwind .container { max-width: 800px; margin: 0 auto; padding: 1rem; }
.no-tailwind .flex { display: flex; }
.no-tailwind .justify-between { justify-content: space-between; }
.no-tailwind .items-center { align-items: center; }
/* ... minimal utility coverage */
```

### Syntax Highlighting Fallback
```css
/* Basic code styling when Prism unavailable */
pre code:not([class*="language-"]) {
    background: var(--bg-deep);
    padding: 1rem;
    border-radius: 0.5rem;
    display: block;
    overflow-x: auto;
}
```

### Font Fallback
```css
.code-font {
    font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', 
                 ui-monospace, 
                 SFMono-Regular, 
                 Menlo, 
                 Monaco, 
                 Consolas, 
                 'Liberation Mono', 
                 'Courier New', 
                 monospace;
}
```

## Offline Detection

```javascript
// Check network status and adjust UI
function checkOnlineStatus() {
    const online = navigator.onLine;
    document.body.classList.toggle('offline', !online);
    
    if (!online) {
        showToast('Offline mode: Some features may be limited', 'info');
    }
}

window.addEventListener('online', checkOnlineStatus);
window.addEventListener('offline', checkOnlineStatus);
```

## CDN Loading States

```javascript
// Track CDN resource loading
const cdnStatus = {
    tailwind: 'pending',
    prism: 'pending',
    fonts: 'pending'
};

// Update status indicators
function updateCdnStatus(resource, status) {
    cdnStatus[resource] = status;
    
    // Show indicator if any CDN failed
    const anyFailed = Object.values(cdnStatus).includes('failed');
    document.getElementById('cdn-status')?.classList.toggle('hidden', !anyFailed);
}
```

## No-CDN Mode (--no-cdns flag)

When user explicitly requests offline-only export:
```rust
pub struct HtmlExportOptions {
    pub enable_cdns: bool,  // Default: true
}

fn generate_head(options: &HtmlExportOptions) -> String {
    if options.enable_cdns {
        // Include CDN references
        include_cdn_resources()
    } else {
        // Only inline resources
        "<!-- CDNs disabled by user -->"
    }
}
```

## Subresource Integrity (SRI)

Add integrity hashes for security:
```html
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/prismjs@1.29.0/prism.min.js"
    integrity="sha384-xxx..."
    crossorigin="anonymous"
    defer></script>
```

## Performance Optimization

### 1. Defer Non-Critical
All CDN scripts use `defer` to not block rendering.

### 2. Media Query Trick
CSS loaded as `media="print"` then switched to `all` on load.

### 3. Preconnect
DNS lookup starts early for CDN domains.

## Testing

### Test Scenarios
1. Full connectivity: All CDNs load
2. Partial failure: Some CDNs timeout
3. Complete offline: No network at all
4. Slow connection: Resources load after content

### Automated Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_html_valid_offline() {
    let html = export_session_no_cdns(&session);
    
    // Validate HTML
    assert!(html.contains("<!DOCTYPE html>"));
    
    // No external references
    assert!(!html.contains("cdn."));
    assert!(!html.contains("googleapis."));
}

#[test]
fn test_html_cdns_optional() {
    let html = export_session_with_cdns(&session);
    
    // CDNs present but with fallbacks
    assert!(html.contains("onerror="));
    assert!(html.contains(".no-tailwind"));
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] HTML renders completely without any CDN
- [ ] CDN resources enhance when available
- [ ] Failed CDN loads don't break the page
- [ ] No-CDN flag produces fully self-contained HTML
- [ ] SRI hashes present for security
- [ ] Loading is non-blocking","notes":"### Testing & Logging
- Unit: CDN URL generation + integrity attributes + fallback logic.
- Integration: simulate offline/blocked CDN and verify inline assets still render.
- E2E: browser tests with network throttling; log resource load failures and fallback path.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-25T20:30:59.693015Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-25T23:32:34.542504Z","closed_at":"2026-01-25T23:32:34.542191Z","close_reason":"Implementation complete: template.rs includes full CDN integration with SRI hashes for Tailwind CSS and Prism.js. Graceful fallback via onerror handlers adding .no-tailwind/.no-prism classes. include_cdn option allows fully offline mode.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["html-export"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-8nnu","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-120r","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-11T06:20:53Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-8nnu","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-bowe","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-11T06:20:53Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-8ns","title":"TUI polish: Midnight Grid theme + agent badges","description":"Implement visual theme (accents, backgrounds), agent badges, role-colored messages, improved result rows, and friendly empty/error states.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-11-23T07:51:12.079664Z","updated_at":"2025-11-23T07:55:33.898794Z","closed_at":"2025-11-23T07:55:33.898794Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["theme","ui"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-8ns","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-6hx","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-11T06:20:54Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-8q8f","title":"Canonicalization pipeline","description":"## Purpose
Implement text canonicalization for consistent embedding input quality.

## Background
Raw agent logs contain noise that hurts embedding quality:
- Markdown formatting (**bold**, [links](url))
- Huge code blocks with repetitive patterns
- Tool call transcripts, progress indicators

Canonicalization produces clean, consistent text. CRITICAL: Must be deterministic for content hashing!

## Algorithm
1. **Unicode normalize (NFC)** ← CRITICAL: ensures visually identical strings hash identically
2. Strip markdown formatting (keep text content)
3. Collapse code blocks: first 20 + last 10 lines, [code omitted] in middle
4. Normalize whitespace (collapse runs, trim)
5. Filter low-signal content ("OK", "Done.")
6. Truncate to MAX_EMBED_CHARS (default 2000)

## Why Unicode Normalization Matters
Without NFC normalization:
- "café" (4 chars, precomposed) ≠ "café" (5 chars, e + combining accent)
- These look identical but produce different hashes
- Would cause duplicate embeddings or missed incremental updates

## Config
- CASS_SEM_MAX_CHARS=2000
- CASS_SEM_CODE_HEAD_LINES=20
- CASS_SEM_CODE_TAIL_LINES=10

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Unicode NFC normalization applied first
- [ ] canonicalize_for_embedding(raw) -> String
- [ ] content_hash(raw) uses canonical text
- [ ] Deterministic (same visual input = same output)
- [ ] Handles edge cases (empty, all-code, unicode combining chars)
- [ ] Test: "café" (precomposed) == "café" (decomposed) after canonicalization

## Depends On
- sem.emb.trait

## References
- Plan: Section 4.6 Canonicalization","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-19T01:23:10.741631Z","updated_at":"2026-01-05T22:59:36.430912Z","closed_at":"2026-01-05T16:04:26.928Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-8q8f","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-vmet","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-11T06:20:54Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"coding_agent_session_search-8sl73","title":"cass introspect --json emits non-deterministic response_schemas block (subset drift between runs)","description":"Discovered while trying to golden-freeze cass introspect --json under bead ibuuh.36 (verification matrix). Two consecutive runs of 'cass introspect --json' against an isolated empty HOME produce BYTE-DIFFERENT output — not just key-reordering, but entries appearing/disappearing from the response_schemas block.

REPRO:
    T1=$(mktemp -d); T2=$(mktemp -d)
    for h in $T1 $T2; do
      XDG_DATA_HOME=$h HOME=$h CODING_AGENT_SEARCH_NO_UPDATE_PROMPT=1 CASS_IGNORE_SOURCES_CONFIG=1 \
        cass introspect --json > /tmp/out.$(basename $h).json
    done
    diff /tmp/out.$(basename $T1).json /tmp/out.$(basename $T2).json | wc -l

Expected: 0 (the schema surface has no runtime-dependent values).
Actual: ~1294 lines differ on my test. The response_schemas map drops/adds individual schemas between runs (e.g. 'diag' present in one, absent in the other).

CAUSE HYPOTHESIS:
The schema registry is built by iterating a std::collections::HashMap (non-deterministic iteration order) AND the building loop appears to be lazy — per-run subsets drift in AND out depending on what subcommand-schema initialization code paths fire during the invocation.

IMPACT:
- Downstream agents that bind to cass programmatically (typed-client generators, validators, capability planners) will silently see different API shapes across invocations.
- Golden-file regression testing on this surface is impossible until it's stable.
- Filed tests/golden_robot_json.rs::introspect_json_matches_golden with #[ignore] + documentation so when this bug is fixed, dropping the ignore provides an immediate regression gate. The golden file itself (tests/golden/robot/introspect.json.golden) is a captured reference for whichever run was the 'UPDATE_GOLDENS=1' pass.

FIX DIRECTION:
Replace HashMap-based schema registry with BTreeMap (or alphabetically sort keys before serialization). Ensure every subcommand's schema is registered up-front, not lazily during first invocation.

FILES:
- src/lib.rs run_introspect / build_introspect_response (or wherever cass introspect --json is built)
- Any schema-collection machinery that uses HashMap<String, ...> in the output path

VERIFICATION:
After fix, tests/golden_robot_json.rs::introspect_json_matches_golden (currently #[ignore]'d) should pass and stay stable across multiple runs. Drop the #[ignore] in the same commit that lands the fix.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T00:00:47.237560808Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:00:47.237560808Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["introspect","robot"]}

Two consecutive runs of 'cass introspect --json' against an isolated empty HOME produce BYTE-DIFFERENT output — not just key-reordering, but entries appearing/disappearing from the response_schemas block.

REPRO:
    T1=$(mktemp -d); T2=$(mktemp -d)
    for h in $T1 $T2; do
      XDG_DATA_HOME=$h HOME=$h CODING_AGENT_SEARCH_NO_UPDATE_PROMPT=1 CASS_IGNORE_SOURCES_CONFIG=1 \
        cass introspect --json > /tmp/out.$(basename $h).json
    done
    diff /tmp/out.$(basename $T1).json /tmp/out.$(basename $T2).json | wc -l

Expected: 0 (the schema surface has no runtime-dependent values).
Actual: ~1294 lines differ on my test. The response_schemas map drops/adds individual schemas between runs (e.g. 'diag' present in one, absent in the other).

CAUSE HYPOTHESIS:
The schema registry is built by iterating a std::collections::HashMap (non-deterministic iteration order) AND the building loop appears to be lazy — per-run subsets drift in AND out depending on what subcommand-schema initialization code paths fire during the invocation.

IMPACT:
- Downstream agents that bind to cass programmatically (typed-client generators, validators, capability planners) will silently see different API shapes across invocations.
- Golden-file regression testing on this surface is impossible until it's stable.
- Filed tests/golden_robot_json.rs::introspect_json_matches_golden with #[ignore] + documentation so when this bug is fixed, dropping the ignore provides an immediate regression gate. The golden file itself (tests/golden/robot/introspect.json.golden) is a captured reference for whichever run was the 'UPDATE_GOLDENS=1' pass.

FIX DIRECTION:
Replace HashMap-based schema registry with BTreeMap (or alphabetically sort keys before serialization). Ensure every subcommand's schema is registered up-front, not lazily during first invocation.

FILES:
- src/lib.rs run_introspect / build_introspect_response (or wherever cass introspect --json is built)
- Any schema-collection machinery that uses HashMap<String, ...> in the output path

VERIFICATION:
After fix, tests/golden_robot_json.rs::introspect_json_matches_golden (currently #[ignore]'d) should pass and stay stable across multiple runs. Drop the #[ignore] in the same commit that lands the fix.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T00:00:47.237560808Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:03:57.045697354Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T00:03:57.045185645Z","close_reason":"Fixed in commit 6a5f159b. IntrospectResponse.response_schemas and build_response_schemas() return type swapped from HashMap to BTreeMap. Two independent runs now produce byte-identical output (diff wc -l = 0). tests/golden_robot_json.rs::introspect_json_matches_golden re-enabled from #[ignore]; 6/6 pass, 3 stable re-runs.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["introspect","robot"]}
 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-8uw2","title":"[BASELINE] Record Performance Baselines Before Optimization","description":"## Overview (from PLAN Section 1.A and Section 2)

Before implementing ANY optimization, we must record comprehensive baseline metrics. This ensures we can accurately measure improvement and detect regressions.

## Required Baseline Measurements

### 1. Benchmark Suite (Section 2.1)
Run and record these benchmarks:
```bash
cargo bench --bench search_perf -- --save-baseline main
cargo bench --bench index_perf -- --save-baseline main
cargo bench --bench runtime_perf -- --save-baseline main
```

**Key Metrics to Record:**
| Benchmark | Current p50 | Target |
|-----------|-------------|--------|
| `vector_index_search_50k` | 56.1 ms | 2-3 ms |
| `vector_index_search_10k` | 11.2 ms | <1 ms |
| `search_latency` (40 convs) | 10.5 µs | <5 µs |
| `wildcard_large_dataset/substring` | 7.5 ms | <2 ms |
| `canonicalize_long_message` | 951 µs | ~300 µs |
| `index_small_batch` | 13.3 ms | maintain |

### 2. Indexing Metrics (Section 2.2)
For a corpus of 3000 conversations × 12 messages = 36,000 messages:
- `elapsed_ms`: p50/p95/p99
- wall clock time
- throughput (messages/s)
- peak RSS (MB)

```bash
# Generate profiling corpus
cargo run --release -- index --full 2>&1 | tee /tmp/index_baseline.log
```

### 3. Search Latency by Query Type (Section 2.3)
Run N=200 iterations for each query type:
| Query Type | p50 | p95 | p99 |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|
| exact (`serialize`) | 3ms | 4ms | 4ms |
| prefix (`ser*`) | 3ms | 3ms | 4ms |
| suffix (`*ialize`) | 6ms | 7ms | 7ms |
| substring (`*erial*`) | 9ms | 10ms | 10ms |
| phrase (`"serialize benchmark"`) | 3ms | 4ms | 4ms |

**IMPORTANT**: CLI-per-search includes cold-open costs. Need to separate `open_ms` vs `query_ms`.

## Recording Location

Store baseline results in:
- Git tag: `perf-baseline-round1`
- Criterion baselines: `target/criterion/*/main/`
- Manual measurements: `docs/perf/baseline_round1.md`

## When to Run

1. **Before starting ANY P0 optimization**
2. **After each optimization** (to create new baseline)
3. **Before creating a PR** (to include before/after comparison)

## Dependencies
- Must complete BEFORE any optimization implementation begins
- Part of Epic: coding_agent_session_search-rq7z","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-10T03:41:41.868679Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-10T06:22:46.913538Z","closed_at":"2026-01-10T06:22:46.913538Z","close_reason":"Baseline benchmarks recorded in docs/perf/baseline_round1.md. Key results: vector_index_search_50k=57.76ms (target: 2-3ms), lexical search_latency=10.7µs, wildcard_large_dataset/substring=6.19ms. Criterion baselines saved to target/criterion/*/main/.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-8w6y","title":"Implement Web Crypto compatible encryption for HTML export","description":"# Task: Web Crypto Compatible Encryption

## Context
Enable optional password-based encryption for exported HTML files. The encryption must
be performed in Rust, but decryption happens client-side using Web Crypto API. This
requires careful alignment of cryptographic parameters.

## Architecture

### Server-Side (Rust) - src/html_export/encryption.rs
1. Generate cryptographic parameters
2. Derive key from password using Argon2id (Rust) -> export as PBKDF2 params for JS
3. Encrypt message payload with AES-256-GCM
4. Embed encrypted blob + decryption script in HTML

### Client-Side (JavaScript)
1. Prompt for password
2. Derive key using PBKDF2 (Web Crypto compatible)
3. Decrypt payload
4. Render conversation

## Key Decision: PBKDF2 for Interoperability
While the project uses Argon2id internally, Web Crypto API doesn't support it natively.
Options:
- **PBKDF2**: Native Web Crypto support, well-tested, slightly weaker than Argon2id
- **scrypt via library**: Requires loading external JS, increases complexity
- **Argon2 WASM**: Complex, large binary size

**Decision**: Use PBKDF2 for HTML export encryption (different from internal Argon2id).
This is acceptable because:
1. The encrypted HTML is for sharing, not long-term storage
2. PBKDF2 with high iterations is still secure for this use case
3. Zero dependencies means smaller file size and better reliability

## Encryption Parameters
```rust
struct HtmlEncryptionConfig {
    // PBKDF2 settings (Web Crypto compatible)
    pbkdf2_iterations: u32,  // 600,000 (OWASP 2024 recommendation)
    salt_bytes: usize,       // 16 bytes
    
    // AES-GCM settings
    aes_key_bits: usize,     // 256
    iv_bytes: usize,         // 12 bytes (96 bits per GCM spec)
    tag_bytes: usize,        // 16 bytes (128 bits)
}

impl Default for HtmlEncryptionConfig {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            pbkdf2_iterations: 600_000,
            salt_bytes: 16,
            aes_key_bits: 256,
            iv_bytes: 12,
            tag_bytes: 16,
        }
    }
}
```

## Rust Encryption Function
```rust
pub fn encrypt_for_html(
    plaintext: &str,
    password: &str,
) -> Result<HtmlEncryptedPayload, EncryptionError> {
    // 1. Generate random salt and IV
    let salt = generate_random_bytes(16);
    let iv = generate_random_bytes(12);
    
    // 2. Derive key using PBKDF2-SHA256
    let key = pbkdf2_sha256(password.as_bytes(), &salt, 600_000, 32);
    
    // 3. Encrypt with AES-256-GCM
    let (ciphertext, tag) = aes_gcm_encrypt(&key, &iv, plaintext.as_bytes(), &[])?;
    
    // 4. Return payload with all params for JS decryption
    Ok(HtmlEncryptedPayload {
        version: 1,
        salt: base64_encode(&salt),
        iv: base64_encode(&iv),
        ciphertext: base64_encode(&ciphertext),
        tag: base64_encode(&tag),
        iterations: 600_000,
    })
}
```

## JavaScript Decryption
```javascript
async function decryptPayload(password) {
    const payload = JSON.parse(document.getElementById('encrypted-data').textContent);
    
    // 1. Decode base64 params
    const salt = base64ToArrayBuffer(payload.salt);
    const iv = base64ToArrayBuffer(payload.iv);
    const ciphertext = base64ToArrayBuffer(payload.ciphertext);
    const tag = base64ToArrayBuffer(payload.tag);
    
    // 2. Import password as key material
    const keyMaterial = await crypto.subtle.importKey(
        'raw',
        new TextEncoder().encode(password),
        'PBKDF2',
        false,
        ['deriveBits', 'deriveKey']
    );
    
    // 3. Derive AES key using PBKDF2
    const key = await crypto.subtle.deriveKey(
        {
            name: 'PBKDF2',
            salt: salt,
            iterations: payload.iterations,
            hash: 'SHA-256'
        },
        keyMaterial,
        { name: 'AES-GCM', length: 256 },
        false,
        ['decrypt']
    );
    
    // 4. Decrypt (Web Crypto expects ciphertext+tag concatenated)
    const combined = new Uint8Array(ciphertext.byteLength + tag.byteLength);
    combined.set(new Uint8Array(ciphertext), 0);
    combined.set(new Uint8Array(tag), ciphertext.byteLength);
    
    const decrypted = await crypto.subtle.decrypt(
        { name: 'AES-GCM', iv: iv },
        key,
        combined
    );
    
    return new TextDecoder().decode(decrypted);
}
```

## Password Modal UI
```html
<div id="decrypt-modal" class="modal">
    <div class="modal-content">
        <h2>🔐 Password Required</h2>
        <p>This conversation is encrypted. Enter the password to view.</p>
        <input type="password" id="password-input" placeholder="Enter password">
        <button onclick="attemptDecrypt()">Decrypt</button>
        <p id="decrypt-error" class="error hidden"></p>
    </div>
</div>
```

## Security Considerations
1. **No password validation**: Wrong password = decryption failure (not explicit error)
2. **Timing attacks**: Use constant-time comparison where possible
3. **Memory clearing**: Clear password from memory after key derivation (JS limitation)
4. **XSS prevention**: Decrypted content sanitized before rendering

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Encryption works with all printable ASCII + Unicode passwords
- [ ] Decryption works in Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge
- [ ] Wrong password shows user-friendly error
- [ ] Correct password reveals full conversation
- [ ] No password hints or validation exposed
- [ ] Cross-platform test: encrypt on Linux, decrypt on macOS","notes":"### Testing & Logging
